Creating XBE Sections

Sections in an XBE image can be created by two different means.

The compiler pragmas dealing with code and data sections (bss_seg, code_seg, const_seg, and data_seg) can be used to place code and data into specific named sections. These sections correspond directly to XBE image sections. Code placed into a named section will be available in a section with that name within the XBE image. The same is true for data sections. The /MERGE linker switch can be used to combine multiple sections into a single section. This switch is useful, for example, to combine a code section and a data section into a single section that contains the code and data necessary for a specific task or set of tasks.

Specifying the /INSERTFILE flag when building an XBE with Image Builder allows you to insert a named file as a section in the image. Specify the name of the file to be inserted as well as the section name, and that file is then available as a section within the XBE. In comparison to a file that is read from a DVD, a file you add as a section is included as part of the XBE image and so is automatically cryptographically signed; its content is verified as intact and unmodified.

Using either method, section names are limited to eight characters or less.


The following example demonstrates the use of compiler pragmas to group code and data into a named section:

// Place the code and data below here into the TESTSEG section.
#pragma code_seg( "TESTSEG" )
#pragma data_seg( "TSEG_RW" )
#pragma bss_seg( "TSEG_RW" )
#pragma const_seg( "TSEG_RD" )
#pragma comment(linker, "/merge:TSEG_RW=TESTSEG")
#pragma comment(linker, "/merge:TSEG_RD=TESTSEG")

These pragmas instruct the compiler to place code into the TESTSEG section and data into the TSEG_RW and TSEG_RD sections. const_seg specifies where constant, initialized data is placed; bss_seg specifies where uninitialized data is placed; data_seg specifies where initialized data is placed; and code_seg specifies where code is placed. The linker's /MERGE switch combines the TESTSEG, TSEG_RW, and TSEG_RD sections into a single section named TESTSEG.

The pragmas above modify the sections where subsequent code and data declarations are placed.

int g_nVal = 1;                         // data
int g_nVal2;                            // bss
const char szString[] = "Test Segment"; // const

These data declarations will be placed into the TSEG_RW and TSEG_RD sections, then merged into the TESTSEG section when the module is linked. The following functions will be placed into the TESTSEG section:

void SetVal2( int n2 )
{
    g_nVal2 = n2;
    return;
}

int GetVal1( void )
{
    return g_nVal;
}

int GetVal2( void )
{
    return g_nVal2;
}

LPCTSTR GetString( void )
{
    return szString;
}

This example creates a collection of functions and data grouped into the TESTSEG section. Use the link -dump -headers command on a compiled .exe (not .xbe) to see reports concerning the sections that a built image contains. For example, the command

link -dump -headers sample.exe

might produce, as part of its output, the following summary that lists the image's sections and the size of each of those sections:

  Summary

          40 .XBLD
         140 .XPP
        46A0 .data
        68C0 .reloc
       18B80 .text
          20 .tls
       26940 D3D
       1D420 D3DX
        1660 D3DX_RW
       5D580 D3D_RW
         900 INIT
        3D20 XGRPH
        1AC0 XGRPH_RW
        99E0 XPP
          40 data
          40 TESTSEG
